title: Linux Networking
description: Linux networking is a vast topic that every infrastructure engineer must understand. From interface configuration to packet filtering, DNS resolution to socket debugging, the tools and concepts covered here are used daily at Enterprise — whether you're debugging a production incident, tuning a load balancer, or designing a VPC topology.

sections.title: Network Interfaces & Configuration
sections.description: Every Linux machine has at least one network interface. Modern Linux uses ip (from iproute2) instead of the deprecated ifconfig and route. At Enterprise, server networking is configured via netplan, cloud-init, or networkd — never by manually editing /etc/network/interfaces on ephemeral instances. Understanding interfaces, addresses, and routing is the foundation.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Modern: ip commands (iproute2) ===
ip addr                              # All interfaces + IPs (equivalent to ifconfig)
ip addr show eth0                    # Single interface
ip -br addr                          # Brief output (compact)
ip link                              # Interface status (up/down, MAC, MTU)
ip route                             # Routing table
ip route show default                # Default gateway
ip neigh                             # ARP table (neighbor cache)

# === Interface management ===
sudo ip link set eth0 up             # Bring interface up
sudo ip link set eth0 down           # Bring interface down
sudo ip addr add 10.0.0.5/24 dev eth0   # Add IP address
sudo ip addr del 10.0.0.5/24 dev eth0   # Remove IP address
sudo ip route add default via 10.0.0.1  # Set default gateway
sudo ip route add 192.168.0.0/16 via 10.0.0.1  # Add static route

# === Legacy ===
ifconfig                             # Interface info (deprecated, still present)
route -n                             # Routing table (deprecated)
netstat -rn                          # Routing table (deprecated)
arp -a                               # ARP table (deprecated)

# === Persistent config (Ubuntu: netplan) ===
# /etc/netplan/00-installer-config.yaml
network:
  version: 2
  ethernets:
    eth0:
      addresses:
        - 10.0.0.5/24
      routes:
        - to: default
          via: 10.0.0.1
      nameservers:
        addresses: [8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1]

# Apply:
sudo netplan apply                    # Apply netplan config

# === MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) ===
# Standard: 1500 bytes. Jumbo frames: 9000 bytes.
# Check: ip link | grep mtu
# Set: sudo ip link set eth0 mtu 9000
# At Enterprise, internal networks often use jumbo frames for storage traffic.

sections.title: Socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof
sections.description: Understanding what ports are listening and what connections are active is essential for debugging. ss (socket statistics) is the modern replacement for netstat — it's faster and shows more information. At Enterprise, these commands are used constantly: 'Is my service listening?', 'Who's connected?', 'Is there a port conflict?'
sections.codeBlock:
# === ss (Socket Statistics) ===
ss -tlnp                             # TCP listening sockets (with process names)
ss -ulnp                             # UDP listening
ss -tanp                             # All TCP connections (including established)
ss -tup                              # All TCP + UDP with processes
ss -s                                # Socket summary (counts by state)
ss -tan state established            # Only established connections
ss -4                                # Only IPv4
ss -6                                # Only IPv6

# === Key fields ===
# Recv-Q, Send-Q  — Queue sizes (non-zero indicates backlog)
# Local Address:Port  — What the socket is bound to
# Peer Address:Port  — Who's connected

# === netstat (legacy, often requires apt install net-tools) ===
netstat -tlnp                         # TCP listening
netstat -tanp                         # All TCP
netstat -i                            # Interface statistics
netstat -s                            # Protocol statistics

# === lsof (list open files) ===
sudo lsof -i :80                      # What's listening on port 80?
sudo lsof -i :443
sudo lsof -p <PID>                    # Open files/sockets for a process
lsof -i -P -n | grep LISTEN           # All listening processes

# === Socket states ===
# LISTEN       — Waiting for connection
# ESTABLISHED  — Active connection
# TIME_WAIT    — Connection closing (waiting for remaining packets)
# CLOSE_WAIT   — Remote closed; local hasn't closed yet
# SYN_SENT     — Trying to connect
# FIN_WAIT1/2  — Closing sequence in progress

# === Common debugging ===
ss -tanp | grep -c ESTABLISHED       # Count active connections
ss -tanp | grep -c TIME_WAIT          # Count TIME_WAIT (high is normal for proxies)
ss -tanp | head -20                   # Check for weird connections

sections.title: DNS Resolution on Linux
sections.description: DNS is how hostnames become IP addresses. Linux uses /etc/resolv.conf for nameserver configuration, /etc/hosts for local overrides, and getent hosts for queries. At Enterprise, internal DNS is critical — services are discovered by name, and DNS failures cause cascading outages.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Name resolution order (nsswitch.conf) ===
grep hosts /etc/nsswitch.conf
# hosts: files dns
# This means: check /etc/hosts first, then DNS

# === /etc/resolv.conf ===
cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 127.0.0.53     # systemd-resolved stub
# nameserver 8.8.8.8
# search internal.example.com  # Auto-append domain for short names
# options rotate         # Round-robin between nameservers
# options timeout:2      # 2-second timeout per query
# options attempts:3     # Retry 3 times before failing

# === DNS queries ===
getent hosts google.com              # System resolver (uses nsswitch)
host google.com                      # Simple DNS lookup
nslookup google.com                  # Legacy query tool
dig google.com                       # Detailed DNS query (best for debugging)

# === dig examples ===
dig google.com                       # A record (IPv4)
dig google.com AAAA                  # IPv6 record
dig google.com MX                    # Mail exchange record
dig google.com NS                    # Nameserver records
dig google.com CNAME                 # Canonical name (alias)
dig -x 8.8.8.8                      # Reverse lookup (PTR)
dig @8.8.8.8 google.com             # Query specific nameserver

# === /etc/hosts ===
cat /etc/hosts
# 127.0.0.1 localhost
# ::1       localhost
# 127.0.0.1 dev-server.internal    # Local override for development

# === systemd-resolved ===
resolvectl status                     # DNS config via systemd-resolved
resolvectl query google.com           # Query via resolved

# === Check which service is using DNS ===
sudo ss -tlnp | grep 53              # DNS listener (systemd-resolved or dnsmasq)

# === DNS troubleshooting ===
dig +trace google.com                 # Trace full resolution path
dig google.com +short                # Just the IP
whois 8.8.8.8                        # IP ownership info

sections.title: Packet Filtering: iptables & nftables
sections.description: Linux has built-in packet filtering via Netfilter. iptables is the legacy interface; nftables is the modern replacement. At Enterprise, cloud security groups handle most firewall rules at the hypervisor level, but host-level iptables/nftables rules are still used for node-local policies, traffic shaping, and Kubernetes network policies.

sections.title: Packet Capture: tcpdump & wireshark
sections.description: When you need to see exactly what's on the wire, packet capture is the ultimate debugging tool. tcpdump is the CLI standard — it's installed on virtually every Linux system. At Enterprise, tcpdump is used for: verifying TLS handshakes, debugging connection resets, analyzing protocol behavior, and security incident investigation.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Basic tcpdump ===
sudo tcpdump -i eth0                  # Capture all traffic on interface
sudo tcpdump -i any                   # Capture on all interfaces
sudo tcpdump -n                       # Don't resolve hostnames (faster, safer)
sudo tcpdump -nn                      # Don't resolve ports either

# === Expresssions (filters) ===
sudo tcpdump -n host 10.0.0.1         # Traffic to/from a specific IP
sudo tcpdump -n src 10.0.0.1         # Source IP
sudo tcpdump -n dst 10.0.0.1         # Destination IP
sudo tcpdump -n port 80              # Specific port
sudo tcpdump -n port 80 or port 443  # Multiple ports
sudo tcpdump -n tcp                  # Only TCP
sudo tcpdump -n udp                  # Only UDP
sudo tcpdump -n icmp                 # Only ICMP (ping)

# === Output control ===
sudo tcpdump -c 100                   # Stop after 100 packets
sudo tcpdump -s 0                     # Capture full packet (default: 68 bytes snaplen)
sudo tcpdump -w capture.pcap          # Save to file (for Wireshark analysis)
sudo tcpdump -r capture.pcap          # Read from file

# === Advanced ===
sudo tcpdump -n 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack == 0'  # SYN packets only
sudo tcpdump -n 'tcp port 443 and (tcp[13] & 0x02 != 0)'  # HTTPS SYN packets

# === One-liner for debugging ===
sudo tcpdump -ni any port 80 -c 100 | grep -i "HTTP/"  # Show HTTP requests

# === tcpdump + Wireshark ===
# Capture on server, analyze on laptop:
# Server: sudo tcpdump -w capture.pcap -i eth0
# Download: scp user@server:capture.pcap .
# Analyze: open in Wireshark

# === Other network debug tools ===
ping -c 5 google.com                  # Basic connectivity test
mtr google.com                        # traceroute + ping in real time (my traceroute)
traceroute google.com                 # Show route path
tracepath google.com                  # Similar, no root needed
curl -v http://example.com            # HTTP debug (verbose headers)
wget --debug http://example.com       # HTTP debug

# === Bandwidth testing ===
iperf3 -c server                      # Network throughput test
iperf3 -s                             # Server mode
nc -vz host 22                        # TCP port connectivity test (netcat)

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'A user reports that 'network is slow.' How do you diagnose?'

A: 'Start broad, then narrow. Check ping for latency and packet loss. Check mtr for the path — is the bottleneck at a specific hop? Check ss -s for socket stats — are there many retransmits or TIME_WAIT connections? Check ip -s link for interface errors (dropped packets, overruns). Check sar -n DEV 1 for bandwidth utilization. If it's an application issue, use tcpdump to analyze the specific traffic. At Enterprise, 'slow network' is almost never the network — it's usually application-level problems (slow queries, insufficient connections, serialization bottlenecks) or DNS issues.'

Q: 'What happens when you type google.com in a browser on Linux?'

A: '(1) Browser checks its cache, then OS DNS cache (systemd-resolved/nscd). (2) /etc/nsswitch.conf says 'files dns' — check /etc/hosts first (no match). (3) Query sent to nameserver in /etc/resolv.conf. (4) If nameserver is 127.0.0.53 (systemd-resolved stub), resolved handles caching and forwarding. (5) DNS resolution returns an IP (e.g., 142.250.80.4). (6) SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK (three-way handshake via connect() syscall). (7) TLS handshake. (8) HTTP request. This is a classic interview question — the depth of your answer shows your understanding of the stack.'

Q: 'What's the difference between iptables and security groups?'

A: 'Security groups are a cloud hypervisor-level firewall — traffic is filtered before it reaches the instance. iptables runs on the instance itself. Security groups are stateless (for Network ACLs) or stateful (for Security Groups in AWS), while iptables is stateful by default with conntrack. At Enterprise, we use security groups for broad traffic policies (e.g., 'only allow HTTP from load balancers') and iptables for fine-grained host-level policies (e.g., 'only allow this specific user to connect to this port'). They complement each other — defense in depth.'

  • Arch Linux Network Configuration — Comprehensive networking guide
  • tcpdump man page — Official tcpdump documentation

learningObjectives: Understand socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof

analogy.title: Networking as a Postal System
analogy.description: IP addresses are street addresses. Ports are apartment numbers. DNS is the phone book translating names to addresses. Routes are the roads. Firewalls (iptables/nftables) are security checkpoints. TCP is registered mail (guaranteed delivery). UDP is a postcard (fire and forget).

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Network Interfaces & Configuration
practiceLabs.task: Run `ip` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ip` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: DNS Resolution on Linux
practiceLabs.task: Run `ifconfig` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ifconfig` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ip` | Network Interfaces & Configuration |
| `sudo` | Network Interfaces & Configuration |
| `ifconfig` | Network Interfaces & Configuration |
| `ss` | Socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof |
| `netstat` | Socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof |
| `sudo` | Socket Diagnostics: ss, netstat & lsof |
| `grep` | DNS Resolution on Linux |
| `cat` | DNS Resolution on Linux |
| `getent` | DNS Resolution on Linux |
| `sudo` | Packet Filtering: iptables & nftables |
| `table` | Packet Filtering: iptables & nftables |
| `chain` | Packet Filtering: iptables & nftables |
| `sudo` | Packet Capture: tcpdump & wireshark |
| `ping` | Packet Capture: tcpdump & wireshark |
| `mtr` | Packet Capture: tcpdump & wireshark |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ip`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: networking operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Network Interfaces & Configuration?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Network Interfaces & Configuration is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ip` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the networking directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a networking directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a networking scenario, your monitoring alerts show ip failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Networking is the most common 'unknown unknown' for engineers moving to infrastructure roles
productionTips: Production Tip:  Always run  ss -tlnp  (not netstat) for socket debugging
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, linux networking is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
